summary: Stancil contrasts JPMorgan’s massive data investment with a hypothetical NBA team’s analytics push to argue that data initiatives work when they’re aimed at clear, concrete problems, and fail when they’re driven by hype and vague promises. He’s skeptical of open-ended “digital transformations,” but sees value in the messy experimentation of the data boom, even if much of it proves wasteful.
money_quotes: Their investment is, as the cliche goes, a $12 billion solution in search of a problem, inspired, it seems, by fear and FOMO.
My skepticism about JPMorgan isn’t exactly about data; it’s about direction.
Open-ended data initiatives suffer from, as Gwen Windflower put it in her new blog, an intention deficit.
Ultimately, that will be the legacy of the modern data stack: the data industry’s creative phase.
Our inventions are real, even if the money that created them was artificial.
key_insight: Data investments deliver value only when tied to clear hypotheses and problems, but even wasteful experiments can yield lasting innovations.
